question: What did Anthropic's CEO say in early 2025 about DeepSeek's claim that its model cost only about $6 million to train?
must_contain: Dario Amodei's late-January 2025 essay response: the ~$6M figure is misleading (it covers only the final training run), DeepSeek did not 'do for $6M what cost US companies billions', and he used the episode to argue for stronger chip export controls.
facts: Amodei's essay 'On DeepSeek and Export Controls' was published in late January 2025
Amodei disclosed Claude 3.5 Sonnet cost 'a few tens of millions' to train, 9-12 months before DeepSeek-V3
He characterized DeepSeek's model as close to US models 7-10 months older, at lower but not revolutionary cost
hardness_note: One search surfaces SemiAnalysis cost estimates and video headlines, not Amodei's actual argument or quotes.
answer:
Now I have the full content. Let me compile the answer.

Here is what Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei said in early 2025 about DeepSeek's claim that its model cost only about $6 million to train:

In a blog post titled **"On DeepSeek and Export Controls"** published on **January 29, 2025** on his website (darioamodei.com), Amodei addressed the claim directly and at length. His key points were:

**1. The $6M figure does not mean DeepSeek did "for $6M what cost US AI companies billions."**  
Amodei wrote: *"DeepSeek does not 'do for $6M what cost US AI companies billions'. I can only speak for Anthropic, but Claude 3.5 Sonnet is a mid-sized model that cost a few $10M's to train (I won't give an exact number)."*

**2. The comparison is misleading on time and cost ratios.**  
He offered what he considered a fair statement: *"DeepSeek produced a model close to the performance of US models 7-10 months older, for a good deal less cost (but not anywhere near the ratios people have suggested)."* He noted that Claude 3.5 Sonnet was trained 9–12 months before DeepSeek-V3 (which was trained in November/December 2024), and that the cost gap, while real, was vastly overstated in popular headlines.

**3. DeepSeek's cost is "on-trend at best."**  
Amodei argued that historical cost reductions for training models run at roughly **4x per year**. By that trend, a model trained a year later should cost about 4x less, and one trained 7–10 months later should cost proportionally less. He wrote: *"DeepSeek-V3 is not a unique breakthrough or something that fundamentally changes the economics of LLMs; it's an expected point on an ongoing cost reduction curve."*

**4. He accepted the $6M figure at face value — but distinguished it from total R&D cost.**  
Amodei explicitly said: *"This is the number quoted in DeepSeek's paper — I am taking it at face value, and not doubting this part of it, only the comparison to US company model training costs, and the distinction between the cost to train a specific model (which is the $6M) and the overall cost of R&D (which is much higher)."* In other words, he did not dispute that the final training run cost ~$5.6M, but he pointed out that this figure excludes the massive prior investment in research, experimentation, and hardware infrastructure (estimates from firms like SemiAnalysis put DeepSeek's total hardware spend at well over $500 million).

**5. The broader strategic takeaway.**  
Rather than seeing DeepSeek's efficiency as a reason to relax US export controls on advanced chips, Amodei argued it actually made export controls *more* existentially important. He wrote: *"In the end, AI companies in the US and other democracies must have better models than those in China if we want to prevail."*

**Sources:**
- [Dario Amodei, "On DeepSeek and Export Controls"](https://darioamodei.com/on-deepseek-and-export-controls)
- [LessWrong copy of the same post](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/BkzeJZCuCyrQrEMAi/dario-amodei-on-deepseek-and-export-controls)

question: How did Linus Torvalds respond to Christoph Hellwig's claim that Rust code had been merged into the Linux kernel over his objections as DMA maintainer?
must_contain: Torvalds's February 2025 rebuttal: the disputed pull request did not touch the DMA layer at all — it merely used the DMA API from a separate subdirectory — and a maintainer does not get to control where his subsystem is used.
facts: The dispute was over Rust bindings calling the kernel's DMA API, February 2025
Torvalds wrote the pull request 'DID NOT TOUCH THE DMA LAYER AT ALL'
Torvalds rejected the position 'as a DMA maintainer I control what the DMA code is used for' as 'not how any of this works'
hardness_note: One search describes the drama's existence but not the content of Torvalds's rebuttal.
answer:
Linus Torvalds responded forcefully and at length on the Linux kernel mailing list on Thursday, February 20, 2025, after Christoph Hellwig claimed that Torvalds had told him privately that he "absolutely is going to merge Rust code over a maintainers objection" [Slashdot](https://linux.slashdot.org/story/25/02/22/0524210/torvalds-rust-kernel-code-isnt-forced-in-over-maintainers-objections). Hellwig, the long-time maintainer of the DMA mapping subsystem, had objected to Rust bindings for the DMA layer being merged into the kernel.

### Linus's core argument

Torvalds began by pointing out a factual error in Hellwig's objection:

> **"the pull request you objected to DID NOT TOUCH THE DMA LAYER AT ALL. It was literally just another user of it, in a completely separate subdirectory, that didn't change the code you maintain in _any_ way, shape, or form."**

He then addressed what he saw as the fundamental misconception:

> **"Honestly, what you have been doing is basically saying 'as a DMA maintainer I control what the DMA code is used for'. And that is not how *any* of this works."**

Torvalds drew an analogy to illustrate the point:

> **"What's next? Saying that particular drivers can't do DMA, because you don't like that device, and as a DMA maintainer you control who can use the DMA code? That's _literally_ exactly what you are trying to do with the Rust code."**

### The "two-way wall of protection"

Torvalds laid out the principle that maintainers cannot simultaneously claim they want nothing to do with Rust *and* veto Rust code that uses their C interfaces:

> **"You can't have it both ways. You can't say 'I want to have nothing to do with Rust', and then in the very next sentence say 'And that means that the Rust code that I will ignore cannot use the C interfaces I maintain'."**

He explained that the "wall of protection" that shields C developers from having to deal with Rust works both ways:

> **"If you don't want to deal with the Rust code, you get no *say* on the Rust code. Put another way: the 'nobody is forced to deal with Rust' does not imply 'everybody is allowed to veto any Rust code'."**

### The choice for maintainers

Torvalds outlined two paths for subsystem maintainers:

- **Engage with the Rust side**: maintainers who *want* to be involved can become maintainers of the Rust bindings as well, and thus have a say in what the bindings look like.
- **Ignore the Rust side**: maintainers who take the "I don't want to deal with Rust" option are not required to learn Rust or deal with Rust code, but they forfeit any say over what happens on the Rust side. When they change C interfaces, the Rust people will have to fix the Rust bindings — that is the promise of the "wall of protection."

He also noted that the line need not be entirely black-and-white, and that in many cases a subsystem maintainer may be aware of the Rust bindings and willing to work with the Rust side without being hugely actively involved.

### Personal tone

Despite the sharp disagreement, Torvalds included a personal note:

> **"I respect you technically, and I like working with you. And no, I am not looking for yes-men, and I like it when you call me out on my bullshit. I say some stupid things at times, there needs to be people who just stand up to me and tell me I'm full of shit. But now I'm calling you out on *YOURS*."**

[Phoronix](https://www.phoronix.com/news/Torvalds-On-Rust-Maintainers) published the full text of the email, and [LWN](https://lwn.net/Articles/1011197) summarized the exchange.

### Aftermath: Hellwig steps down as DMA maintainer

Following Torvalds's intervention, Christoph Hellwig stepped down as maintainer of the DMA mapping subsystem. In the Linux 6.14 MAINTAINERS file update, Hellwig wrote that "Marek has graciously offered to maintain the dma-mapping tree," and Marek Szyprowski took over sole maintainership of that subsystem. Hellwig continues to maintain other kernel areas, including the NVMe driver, NVMe target driver, personality handling, and the FreeVXFS file-system [Phoronix on Hellwig stepping down](https://www.phoronix.com/news/Hellwig-DMA-Helpers-Removed).

[Note: stopped early — time limit reached]

question: How did Sam Altman publicly respond to Elon Musk's $97.4 billion takeover bid for OpenAI, and what counter-offer did he joke about?
must_contain: Altman rejected the February 2025 bid on X with a joke counter-offer to buy Twitter for $9.74 billion, and said Musk was probably just trying to slow OpenAI down.
facts: Altman posted 'no thank you but we will buy twitter for $9.74 billion if you want'
The Musk-led bid was $97.4 billion, February 2025
At the Paris AI summit Altman told Bloomberg Musk was 'probably just trying to slow us down' and said he took the offer 'not particularly' seriously
hardness_note: One search shows the rejection happened but the $9.74B Twitter joke never appears in snippets.
answer:
Sam Altman responded publicly to Elon Musk's $97.4 billion takeover bid on **February 10, 2025**, posting on Musk's own platform X (formerly Twitter):

> **"no thank you but we will buy twitter for $9.74 billion if you want."** [Sam Altman's X post](https://x.com/sama/status/1889059531625464090?lang=en)

The joke counter-offer was a pointed reference to the fact that Musk personally bought Twitter in 2022 for **$44 billion** — so Altman's "offer" of $9.74 billion was exactly **one-tenth** of Musk's $97.4 billion bid for OpenAI, sarcastically implying that OpenAI was worth far more to Musk than Twitter was worth to Altman. [Yahoo Finance](https://finance.yahoo.com/news/elon-musk-reportedly-offers-974-billion-for-openai-sam-altman-says-no-thank-you-215634193.html) | [NBC News](https://www.nbcnews.com/business/business-news/elon-musk-investors-offering-97point4-billion-control-openai-rcna191572)

Musk fired back minutes later, calling Altman a **"swindler"** in a reply, and in a separate reply called him **"Scam Altman."** [Variety](https://variety.com/2025/biz/news/open-ai-sam-altman-elon-musk-reject-ai-1236303087) | [NBC News](https://www.nbcnews.com/business/business-news/elon-musk-investors-offering-97point4-billion-control-openai-rcna191572)

The bid itself — led by Musk alongside Valor Equity Partners, Baron Capital, 8VC, and Ari Emanuel's investment fund, with xAI potentially merging with OpenAI if the deal went through — was for the **nonprofit that oversees OpenAI**. Altman's rejection was formalized internally in a staff memo stating the board had no interest in the "supposed bid." [Variety](https://variety.com/2025/biz/news/open-ai-sam-altman-elon-musk-reject-ai-1236303087)

Months later, in April 2025, OpenAI filed a countersuit against Musk alleging the $97.375 billion offer was a **"sham bid"** designed to disrupt OpenAI's fundraising, and that the specific figure appeared to be nothing more than "a comedic reference to Musk's favorite sci-fi series" — specifically the character **974 Praf** from Iain Banks's 2000 novel *Look to Windward*. [Mashable](https://mashable.com/article/openai-musk-lawsuit-countersuit-takeover-bid)
